%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-plan" data-visual-plan-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
# 4.12-4 Chebyshev Filters

> **Section Objective:** Chebyshev lowpass filters accept controlled passband ripple to achieve a sharper transition band and stronger stopband attenuation than equal-order Butterworth filters.

---

## Concepts In This Section

- Chebyshev magnitude response
- Passband ripple factor
- Chebyshev polynomial recursion
- Stable pole locations
- MATLAB design workflow

## 1. Chebyshev Magnitude Response

This formula gives the **squared magnitude** of an order-\(N\) Chebyshev lowpass filter.

**Symbol guide:**
- \(H_C(j\omega)\) — the complex frequency response of the Chebyshev filter
- \(\epsilon\) — the ripple factor; controls how deep the passband ripple is
- \(C_N\) — the degree-\(N\) Chebyshev polynomial evaluated at normalized frequency \(\omega/\omega_c\)
- \(N\) — filter order; higher order means more ripple extrema and a sharper transition
- \(\omega_c\) — the cutoff (passband-edge) frequency

**When to use it:** Any exam question about Chebyshev ripple, cutoff behavior, or magnitude response shape starts here.

**Key reading rule:**
- Inside the passband \((\omega \le \omega_c)\): the response is **equiripple** — it oscillates between 1 and \(1/(1+\epsilon^2)\).
- Outside the passband \((\omega > \omega_c)\): the response **decreases monotonically**.

**Minimal example:** For \(N = 8\), the passband contains a total of 8 maxima and minima.

#### WARNING
Do **not** assume \(\omega_c\) is automatically the 3 dB frequency. For Chebyshev filters, \(\omega_c\) marks the passband edge, which is generally not the 3 dB point.

$$|H_C(j\omega)|^2 = \frac{1}{1 + \epsilon^2 C_N^2(\omega/\omega_c)}$$

## 2. Chebyshev polynomials

**Symbol guide:**
- \(R\) — allowable passband ripple in decibels (given in the design specification)
- \(\epsilon\) — the ripple factor that enters the magnitude-squared formula above

**Exam trigger:** If a problem states ripple in dB, convert to \(\epsilon\) immediately before doing anything else.

**Passband gain facts:**
- Maximum passband gain: \(1\)
- Minimum passband gain: \(1/\sqrt{1+\epsilon^2}\)

**Parity detail:** For odd \(N\), the dc gain equals 1. For even \(N\), the dc gain equals \(1/\sqrt{1+\epsilon^2}\).

#### COMMON MISUSE
Reducing \(R\) reduces ripple depth, but it also weakens the Chebyshev performance advantage — a smaller \(\epsilon\) means a less sharp transition relative to Butterworth.

$$\epsilon = \sqrt{10^{R/10} - 1}$$

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="interactive_demo" data-teaching-role="concept_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div><div class="kc-interactive-demo" data-demo-b64=""></div>%%KC_END%%

## 3. Stable Chebyshev poles

\(C_N(x)\) is the **degree-\(N\) Chebyshev polynomial** that creates the equiripple passband shape. When used in the filter response, \(x = \omega/\omega_c\) is the normalized frequency.

**Two useful forms:**
- **Cosine form** (shown above): reveals why the passband oscillates — \(\cos(N\cos^{-1}x)\) oscillates between \(-1\) and \(1\) for \(|x| \le 1\), producing the equiripple behavior.
- **Hyperbolic form** \(C_N(x) = \cosh(N\cosh^{-1}x)\): describes the monotonic growth outside the passband where \(|x| > 1\).

**Exam trigger:** If a problem asks why an order-\(N\) Chebyshev filter has \(N\) extrema in the passband, point directly to the degree-\(N\) polynomial.

#### COMMON MISUSE
Do not treat \(C_N\) as an arbitrary constant. It is a polynomial evaluated at the normalized frequency \(\omega/\omega_c\), and its degree \(N\) directly controls the ripple count.

$$C_N(x) = \cos\!\left(N \cos^{-1} x\right)$$

$$C_N(x) = 2x\,C_{N-1}(x) - C_{N-2}(x)$$
*This **recurrence relation** is the practical way to generate Chebyshev polynomial coefficients without evaluating the cosine form directly.

**Starting values:** \(C_0(x) = 1\) and \(C_1(x) = x\).

**Worked examples:**

$$C_2(x) = 2x \cdot x - 1 = 2x^2 - 1$$

$$C_3(x) = 2x(2x^2 - 1) - x = 4x^3 - 3x$$

**When to use it:** Any MATLAB or hand calculation that asks for the coefficients of \(C_N(x)\) uses this recurrence.

#### COMMON MISUSE
Each step depends on the **previous two** polynomials \(C_{N-1}\) and \(C_{N-2}\), not only the immediately preceding one. Forgetting \(C_{N-2}\) gives wrong coefficients.*

## 3. Stable Chebyshev Poles

The causal stable Chebyshev filter is built by selecting the \(N\) **left-half-plane poles** from the full set of \(2N\) roots.

**Supporting quantities:**

$$\phi_k = \frac{(2k-1)\pi}{2N}, \quad k = 1, 2, \ldots, N$$

$$\xi = \frac{1}{N}\sinh^{-1}\!\left(\frac{1}{\epsilon}\right)$$

**Why the formula places poles in the left half-plane:** The real part of \(p_k\) is \(-\omega_c \sinh(\xi)\sin(\phi_k)\). Since \(\sinh(\xi) > 0\) and \(\sin(\phi_k) > 0\) for \(k = 1, \ldots, N\), the real part is always **negative**, guaranteeing left-half-plane placement.

**Visual fact:** Chebyshev poles lie on an **ellipse** in the complex plane, unlike Butterworth poles, which lie on a circle. The ellipse semi-axes are \(\omega_c\sinh(\xi)\) (real axis) and \(\omega_c\cosh(\xi)\) (imaginary axis).

#### COMMON MISUSE
Choosing all \(2N\) roots, or any right-half-plane roots, produces an unstable or noncausal design. Always select only the \(N\) left-half-plane poles.

$$p_k = \omega_c\!\left[-\sinh(\xi)\sin(\phi_k) + j\cosh(\xi)\cos(\phi_k)\right]$$

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="interactive_demo" data-teaching-role="exam_pattern_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div><div class="kc-interactive-demo" data-demo-b64=""></div>%%KC_END%%

---
**📌 Key Takeaways**
- Chebyshev LPF trades equiripple passband for sharper transition than Butterworth: \(|H_C(j\omega)|^2 = 1/(1+\epsilon^2 C_N^2(\omega/\omega_c))\).
- Convert dB ripple to ripple factor first: \(\epsilon = \sqrt{10^{R/10}-1}\); max gain = 1, min passband gain = \(1/\sqrt{1+\epsilon^2}\).
- Chebyshev polynomial \(C_N(x) = \cos(N\cos^{-1}x)\) gives \(N\) passband extrema; stopband is monotonically decreasing.
- Generate coefficients by recursion: \(C_N(x) = 2x C_{N-1}(x) - C_{N-2}(x)\), with \(C_0=1\), \(C_1=x\), giving \(C_2=2x^2-1\), \(C_3=4x^3-3x\).
- Stable causal design uses the \(N\) left-half-plane ellipse poles: \(p_k = \omega_c[-\sinh(\xi)\sin(\phi_k)+j\cosh(\xi)\cos(\phi_k)]\); \(\omega_c\) is not the 3 dB point.

*Next, use these filter ideas to compare practical continuous-time filter implementations.*
